title: Protocol for Small Mustelid Carcass Collection, Handling, and Storage
author: Andrin C. Dürst
materials: Use clean, sterilized tools (scissors, scalpel, forceps).
Taxon code Standardized codes for taxon codes include:
Use durable, waterproof and ethanol-proof labels .

steps.title: Upon receipt, the coordinator or trained personnel will
steps.instruction: Upon receipt, the coordinator or trained personnel will: Assign a unique label ID to the carcass (see section 3 for the ID system).

steps.title: 2. Take morphological measurements (see Fig. 1)
steps.instruction: 2. Take morphological measurements (see Fig. 1): Figure 1: Example setup of the lab bench during morphological measurements and tissue sampling of small carnivore carcasses.
3. Determine sex (male/female) and age class (juvenile, subadult, adult).

steps.title: Take a tissue sample for genetic analysis
steps.instruction: Take a tissue sample for genetic analysis.

steps.title: For convenience and ease of access,...
steps.instruction: For convenience and ease of access, cutting a piece of the tongue is recommended as it contains muscle tissue and is relatively easy to sample.

steps.title: If the tongue is not in...
steps.instruction: If the tongue is not in good condition, alternative tissue sources include:

steps.title: - Back muscle (can be accessed...
steps.instruction: - Back muscle (can be accessed through the ventral cavity, often less decomposed).

steps.title: Cut small pieces (e
steps.instruction: Cut small pieces (e.g. minimum of 0.5–1 cm 3 ) and immediately preserve.

steps.title: Preserve in ethanol (95–100%) using small containers (e
steps.instruction: Preserve in ethanol (95–100%) using small containers (e.g. screw-cap microcentrifuge tubes).

steps.title: Check guideline for genetic sampling (Dürst 2025, DOI
steps.instruction: Check guideline for genetic sampling (Dürst 2025, DOI: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.eq2ly4wmplx9/v1) of small mustelids for details on sampling and storing tissue.

steps.title: If disease or pathogen analysis is...
steps.instruction: If disease or pathogen analysis is planned, this is the appropriate time to take the necessary samples or hand the carcass to veterinary specialists.

steps.title: This must be done before freezing to ensure pathogen viability
steps.instruction: This must be done before freezing to ensure pathogen viability.

steps.title: Details of pathogen sampling are outside...
steps.instruction: Details of pathogen sampling are outside the scope of this protocol, but should be coordinated with veterinary or disease specialists.

steps.title: If no pathogen analysis is planned...
steps.instruction: If no pathogen analysis is planned (or has been done), the carcass should be frozen for long-term storage .

steps.title: Store frozen at a minimum of -20 °C
steps.instruction: Store frozen at a minimum of -20 °C . Colder temperatures (e.g., -80 °C) are preferable if facilities allow.

steps.title: Ensure carcasses are double-bagged with clear...
steps.instruction: Ensure carcasses are double-bagged with clear labelling on the outer bag, including sample ID, date, and location information.

steps.title: Avoid thawing and refreezing cycles to maintain sample integrity
steps.instruction: Avoid thawing and refreezing cycles to maintain sample integrity.

steps.title: Store tissue samples in screw-cap microtubes...
steps.instruction: Store tissue samples in screw-cap microtubes filled with �>95% ethanol (preferably 99%).

steps.title: Keep these samples at -20 °C...
steps.instruction: Keep these samples at -20 °C or lower for long-term preservation.

steps.title: Ensure labels are ethanol-proof
steps.instruction: Ensure labels are ethanol-proof.

steps.title: To maintain consistency across all collections...
steps.instruction: To maintain consistency across all collections and enable easy tracking and data sharing,use a standardized unique ID format : [CountryCode]-[Year]-[InstitutionCode]-[TaxonCode]-[SampleType]-[SampleNumber][LetterSuffix]

steps.title: Sources of carcasses Carcasses will most...
steps.instruction: Sources of carcasses Carcasses will most commonly be found opportunistic as: Roadkill Kills by domestic cats (By)catch in trapping activities Incidental finds during fieldwork or recreational activities Animals that died in wildlife care or rehabilitation centres Eradication programs While roadkill and cat kills are expected to be the main sources, collectors should remain open to other sources or may plan active sampling (e.g. along roads).

steps.title: Data collection and recording When collecting...
steps.instruction: Data collection and recording When collecting carcasses, it is crucial torecord essential data. At a minimum, record: GPS coordinates of the find (preferably in WGS84 format) Date of collection Additional useful data includes: Species identification and certainty of identification Cause of death (clearly state if assumed or confirmed) Condition of the carcass (fresh, decomposed, scavenged, etc.) Name and contact information of the finder/collector Notes about habitat and any other remarks Photographs of the carcass and surroundings (optional but recommended)

steps.title: Safety and hygiene Always wear disposable...
steps.instruction: Safety and hygiene Always wear disposable gloves when handling carcasses to avoid contamination and potential pathogen exposure. If gloves are unavailable, a plastic bag turned inside out can be used as animprovised glove. Use hand sanitizer after handling the carcass and before touching anything else. Wash your hands ifpossible. Be mindful of traffic safety when collecting roadkill; take appropriate precautions to avoid accidents.

steps.title: Place the carcass into a plastic...
steps.instruction: Place the carcass into a plastic bag and tie it closed with a secure knot.

steps.title: Place that bag into a second...
steps.instruction: Place that bag into a second plastic bag and tie it closed as well. This double-bagging reduces the risk of contamination and leakage.

steps.title: Insert a label or note with the collected metadata (minimum
steps.instruction: Insert a label or note with the collected metadata (minimum: label ID, date, GPS coordinates) between the two bags to prevent sample misidentification if outer labels become damaged.

steps.title: Avoid reusing bags or containers from...
steps.instruction: Avoid reusing bags or containers from previous collections to prevent cross-contamination.

steps.title: If possible, keep the carcass cool...
steps.instruction: If possible, keep the carcass cool in a cool box or refrigerator until it can be delivered to the local collection coordinator. This should be done on the same day or overnight.

steps.title: Freezing the carcass immediately after collection...
steps.instruction: Freezing the carcass immediately after collection is discouraged if there is any intention of conducting pathogen or disease analyses later because freezing can destroy or reduce the viability of pathogens.

steps.title: If transfer to the collection point...
steps.instruction: If transfer to the collection point is delayed or not possible, freezing is the only alternative to prevent carcass degradation, but it should be considered a last resort after consultation with the coordinator.

steps.title: Receiving and transfer to the coordinator
steps.instruction: Receiving and transfer to the coordinator: The ideal situation is to have local collection points where carcasses can be brought for initial handling. Usually, this is managed by a person responsible for mustelid research in that area, hereafter called the coordinator . The coordinator may be responsible for a local or national collection effort.

steps.title: Send packages in the afternoon just...
steps.instruction: Send packages in the afternoon just before the parcel drop-off deadline for next-day delivery.

steps.title: Select the next-day delivery option to minimize transit time
steps.instruction: Select the next-day delivery option to minimize transit time.

steps.title: Do not send before the weekends...
steps.instruction: Do not send before the weekends to avoid waiting time in transit.

steps.title: Use cool packs and/or insulated packaging (e
steps.instruction: Use cool packs and/or insulated packaging (e.g., bubble wrap, multiple paper layers) to keep samples cool during shipping.

steps.title: Confirm that the delivery address is...
steps.instruction: Confirm that the delivery address is correct and someone is available to receive the package (or a 24h accessible mailbox).

steps.title: If feasible, someone from the coordinator’s...
steps.instruction: If feasible, someone from the coordinator’s team can pick up the sample from the finder.

steps.title: Multiple local collection points (e
steps.instruction: Multiple local collection points (e.g., museums or vets) may be a possible solution to facilitate logistics in remote places.

steps.title: Figure 2
steps.instruction: Figure 2: Example of a carcass that is double-bagged, labelled, and prepared for storage in a freezer.

steps.title: Maintain a secure, backed-up digital database...
steps.instruction: Maintain a secure, backed-up digital database with detailed metadata for all samples.

steps.title: Update the database whenever samples are...
steps.instruction: Update the database whenever samples are moved, processed, or used in analyses.

steps.title: Example
steps.instruction: Example: CH-2025-NMBEX-MuNi-CAR-0071aThis represents: CH = Switzerland (ISO 3166-1alpha-2 country code) 2025 = Year of collection NMBEX = Institution code (e.g. Natural history museum of Bern, completed with an X) MuNi = Taxon code (e.g. Mustela nivalis ) CAR = Sample type (e.g. carcass) 0071a = Sample number 71, subsample “a” (e.g. a carcass will most likely be the first sample taken)

steps.title: Sample type abbreviations Standardized codes for common sample types include:
steps.instruction: Sample type abbreviations Standardized codes for common sample types include:

steps.title: Suffixes for sub-samples
steps.instruction: Suffixes for sub-samples: When multiple samples of the same or different types are taken from a single individual, append a letter suffix (a, b, c, etc.) to indicate that they are subsamples from the same source (individual).

steps.title: Preferably write labels with a pencil...
steps.instruction: Preferably write labels with a pencil or ethanol-proof ink to ensure longevity in ethanol and freezing conditions.

steps.title: Make sure the metadata recorded in...
steps.instruction: Make sure the metadata recorded in the database matches exactly the physical labels on the sample tubes and carcass bags.

steps.title: Include the sample ID, date and...
steps.instruction: Include the sample ID, date and GPS coordinates on the label to ensure that, even in the case of an error or mix-up, the essential information remains linked to the specimen.

steps.title: Metadata record See Appendix A of...
steps.instruction: Metadata record See Appendix A of the guidelines for genetic sampling of small mustelids for details and a complete list of metadata fields.

steps.title: Alongside physical labelling (ID), maintain a...
steps.instruction: Alongside physical labelling (ID), maintain a digital database of all samples with detailed metadata fields including: Unique sample ID Date (and time) of collection GPS coordinates (WGS84) Species identification and certainty Cause of death (confirmed or assumed) Condition of the sample (carcass) Collector/finder name and contact Storage location (Institution) Morphometrics (Bodysize, Weight, Sex, etc.; if available) Comments and photos (if necessary)

steps.title: An Excel template for metadata entry...
steps.instruction: An Excel template for metadata entry will be provided upon request for uniform data collection and easy sharing.

steps.title: Options to transport the specimen to...
steps.instruction: Options to transport the specimen to the coordinator/collection: The finder or collector can bring the sample to the coordinator in person. The sample can be sent by mail or courier — if so, ensure the following:

steps.title: This is an ideal time to collect tissue samples (e
steps.instruction: This is an ideal time to collect tissue samples (e.g. for genetic analysis), as it helps avoid repeated freezing and thawing later. Once sampling is complete, carcasses can be transferred to the freezer for long-term storage, while the tissue sample remains available for immediate or future use.

steps.title: - Heart muscle (preferred tissue for...
steps.instruction: - Heart muscle (preferred tissue for genetic analysis, but less accessible).

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: If bringing or picking up samples, transporting them in a cool box is preferable, but if not possible, transport without cooling is acceptable if the time between collection and processing is short (e.g. a few hours).

steps.title: Important
steps.instruction: Important: If the carcass is intended for taxidermy , take tissue samples in a way that will not be visible on the prepared specimen later on (e.g. tongue ).

steps.title: Important
steps.instruction: Important: Use of double-bagging for carcasses and placement of a note with the sample ID, collection date, and GPS coordinates alongside the sample reduces the risk of misidentification if labels become detached or mixed up.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: Even though other sample types are not covered in this protocol, the ID structure is designed to accommodate additional types for completeness.
